epics.title: Implementation: Agent Service
epics.description: Build the TypeScript agent service that manages SDK agent sessions. This is the backend process that spawns agents, routes messages, tracks state, and exposes a transport-agnostic API over Unix socket.
epics.body:
## What We Know

The full design is captured in 13 requirement assets under epic [iduwan11]. Key references:

- **[i5eisc9o] Agent Service API** — AgentManager class, methods, ManagedAgent struct, internal architecture
- **[4o3g4qf3] Unix Socket Protocol** — NDJSON wire format, all commands/events/error codes
- **[ik1yp2tc] Agent State Machine, Health & Cutoff** — states, transitions, health detection, message delivery triggers, polite/hard cutoff flow
- **[f5z68c4v] Trace Log & State Recovery** — 13 entry types, query API, crash recovery from trace
- **[il2m3sl0] Event Subscriptions (Manager-Only)** — bounded delivery, auto-subscriptions, loop prevention
- **[0l0qkut7] Agent-Side Tools** — 5 tools registered per agent via customTools
- **[gzoe99o0] System Prompt Assembly** — per-turn rebuild, address book from ACL, unattended instructions
- **[n2j36dl7] Settings & Config Schema** — full schemas, auto-scaffolding, default merging
- **[a3m0qqbu] Reference File Structure** — service source layout, all files and who creates them
- **[0osnjjl4] Extension Hooks & Lifecycle** — SDK session hooks, tool execute handlers, health timer, settings watcher

## Key Architecture Decisions

- Standalone process, survives manager restart
- SDK `createAgentSession` for all agents, in-process (not tmux)
- `systemPromptOverride` is a function — rebuilt per turn (live SYSTEM.md, live ACL)
- `fs.watch()` on settings.json for live config/ACL updates
- Messages trigger agent turns (`session.prompt()` when idle)
- Agents are unattended, message-driven workers — no subscriptions
- Subscriptions are manager-only, delivered via socket events
- Auto-scaffolding: creates .pi/agents/ + settings.json + all files with defaults on first run
- Missing fields merged into existing files on every load

## Implementation Scope

The service is a standalone npm package (`pi-agent-service`) with:
- CLI entry point (`pi-agent-service --project /path`)
- AgentManager core class
- MessageRouter (ACL, queue, threads, delivery)
- TraceWriter (append-only JSONL)
- StateRecovery (rebuild from trace)
- CutoffMonitor + HealthMonitor
- SubscriptionManager (manager-only)
- SettingsLoader (read + watch)
- Agent tools factory (5 customTools per agent)
- Prompt builder (system prompt assembly function)
- Unix socket adapter (NDJSON protocol)

todos.text: Core AgentManager class (transport-agnostic API)
MessageQueue with threading, priority, delivery tracking
Trace — append-only JSONL audit log
Agent state machine (offline/idle/working) with health monitoring

context_handoff tool + session.newSession() + continue flow
Agent-side tools: send_message, context_handoff, list_messages, list_threads, check_status
System prompt assembly (SYSTEM.md + coding guidelines + unattended instructions)
Unix socket transport adapter (JSONL protocol)
Service lifecycle: PID file, singleton, graceful shutdown, state recovery from trace
Default subscriptions: agent_message, agent_state_change
Event subscription system (file_modified, json_value_change, log_error)
settings.json loading and per-agent config resolution

All agent-side tools work (send_message, handoff, list_messages, check_status)

epics.title: Implementation: Manager Extension
epics.description: Build the pi extension loaded by the manager's pi session. Connects to the agent service via Unix socket, registers tools grouped by mode, manages subscriptions, status bar, and context cutoff for the manager itself.
epics.body:
## What We Know

The full design is captured in 13 requirement assets under epic [iduwan11]. Key references:

- **[5c5nibh6] Manager Tools & Modes** — 5 modes, ~40 tools, mode-specific prompt injection, status bar
- **[8ikfn8hs] Manager Loop Mode** — cycle lifecycle, keep-alive hook, countdown, wake-on-event, LOOP_INSTRUCTIONS.md, DEFERRED.md
- **[0osnjjl4] Extension Hooks & Lifecycle** — session_start (10-step init), before_agent_start (prompt rebuild), agent_end (loop keep-alive), socket event listeners
- **[il2m3sl0] Event Subscriptions (Manager-Only)** — auto-subscriptions, bounded delivery
- **[gzoe99o0] System Prompt Assembly** — manager prompt: SYSTEM.md + mode instructions + address book
- **[a3m0qqbu] Reference File Structure** — extension source layout, all files

## Key Architecture Decisions

- Pi extension loaded into user's normal TUI session
- Connects to agent service via Unix socket (auto-starts service if needed)
- Tools are thin wrappers → validate params → send protocol command → format response
- Mode switching dynamically enables/disables tools
- System prompt rebuilt every turn via `before_agent_start`
- Loop mode has keep-alive hook preventing idle without `manager_loop_next`
- Auto-scaffolding: creates .pi/agents/ + settings.json + LOOP_INSTRUCTIONS.md + DEFERRED.md
- manager-state.json persists mode + loop state + subscriptions across context restarts

Dynamic tool registration/deregistration on mode switch

issues.title: Spike: SDK createAgentSession capabilities
issues.description:
Test what `createAgentSession` from `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent` SDK can actually do. **If SDK works well, it eliminates the need for PI_AGENT_NAME env var entirely** — the service manages identity.

Test each capability with a small script:
1. Can we load custom extensions into an SDK session?
2. Can we load packages and skills?
3. Can we stream output (token by token)?
4. Can we inject user messages programmatically?
5. Can we trigger reload/new-session/compaction?
6. Can we read the full conversation context?
7. What happens on context cutoff — can we catch and handle it?
8. Can multiple SDK sessions run in one Node process?
9. Can we set/override system prompt per session?
10. Can we control tool availability per session?

Document YES/NO with code samples for each.

research.content:
## SDK Capabilities (from docs)

**All critical capabilities confirmed in API:**
- ✅ `createAgentSession` with custom tools, extensions, system prompt override
- ✅ `DefaultResourceLoader` with `additionalExtensionPaths` and `extensionFactories`
- ✅ `systemPromptOverride` — can set per-agent personality
- ✅ `session.prompt()` / `session.followUp()` — inject messages programmatically
- ✅ `session.subscribe()` — full event streaming (text deltas, tool calls, lifecycle)
- ✅ `session.newSession()` — context restart programmatically
- ✅ `session.compact()` — manual compaction trigger
- ✅ `SessionManager.inMemory()` — no filesystem session needed
- ✅ `SettingsManager.inMemory()` — full config control
- ✅ Custom tools via `customTools: ToolDefinition[]`
- ✅ `createEventBus()` — inter-extension communication from outside

**This means:** We can spawn multiple `AgentSession` instances in one Node process, each with different system prompts, tools, and settings. No env vars needed. The service IS the orchestrator.

## Spike Result: ALL CRITICAL CAPABILITIES CONFIRMED ✅

Ran `spike-sdk/main.ts` — two agents (Alice & Bob) exchanged 5+ rounds of messages successfully.

### Confirmed capabilities:
1. ✅ **Multiple AgentSessions in one process** — Alice and Bob ran concurrently
2. ✅ **Different system prompts per session** — `systemPromptOverride` works perfectly
3. ✅ **Custom tools per session** — each agent got personalized send_message/check_messages tools
4. ✅ **Programmatic prompting** — `session.prompt()` drives each agent turn
5. ✅ **Event subscription** — `session.subscribe()` streams all lifecycle events
6. ✅ **No coding tools needed** — `tools: []` works, agents only had custom tools
7. ✅ **In-memory sessions** — `SessionManager.inMemory()` works, no filesystem sessions
8. ✅ **Filesystem mailbox** — read-once messaging works perfectly
9. ✅ **Clean disposal** — `session.dispose()` cleans up

### Key observation:
- Bob was supposed to be "grumpy" but the system prompt personality was somewhat ignored — both agents were quite friendly. This is a prompt engineering issue, not an SDK limitation.
- Total: 28 Alice turns, 30 Bob turns — the SDK managed all state cleanly.

### Architecture implication:
**No need for PI_AGENT_NAME, tmux, RPC, or launch.sh for agent spawning.** A single TypeScript service using the SDK can manage all agents programmatically. The manager extension just needs tools to interact with this service.

## Complete SDK Capabilities Findings

### 1. Multiple Sessions in One Process ✅
Two `AgentSession` instances (Alice & Bob) ran concurrently via `Promise.all`. No conflicts, no shared state issues. Each session is fully independent.

### 2. Custom System Prompts per Session ✅
`systemPromptOverride` in `DefaultResourceLoader` or directly in `createAgentSession` options. Each agent gets a unique personality/role.

### 3. Custom Tools per Session ✅
`customTools: ToolDefinition[]` — each session gets its own tool set. Agents can have completely different capabilities. `tools: []` removes all built-in coding tools.

### 4. Extension Loading ✅
`DefaultResourceLoader` with `additionalExtensionPaths` loads real extensions (tested with web-search). Extensions register tools normally via `pi.registerTool()` and they work.

### 5. Programmatic Prompting ✅
- `session.prompt(text)` — send and wait for completion
- `session.steer(text)` — interrupt: delivered after current tool
- `session.followUp(text)` — queued: delivered when agent finishes

### 6. Fully Async / Parallel ✅
`Promise.all([agentA.prompt(...), agentB.prompt(...)])` works. Agents run truly simultaneously. Filesystem mailbox handles concurrent reads/writes.

### 7. Token Streaming ✅
`session.subscribe()` fires `message_update` events with `text_delta` for every token. Timestamps allow measuring token gaps for stuck-agent detection. Events:
- `turn_start` / `turn_end` — per LLM response
- `tool_execution_start` / `tool_execution_end` — per tool call
- `agent_start` / `agent_end` — per full prompt cycle
- `message_start` / `message_end` — per message

### 8. Context Usage ✅
`session.getContextUsage()` returns:
```json
{ "tokens": 4645, "contextWindow": 1000000, "percent": 0.46 }
```
Real-time context window percentage — perfect for cutoff detection.

### 9. Session Stats ✅
`session.getSessionStats()` returns:
```json
{
  "userMessages": 2, "assistantMessages": 2,
  "toolCalls": 0, "toolResults": 0, "totalMessages": 4,
  "tokens": { "input": 6, "output": 17, "cacheRead": 9226, "total": 9275 },
  "cost": 0.00523
}
```

### 10. Per-Message Usage ✅
Each assistant message has `usage` field:
```json
{ "input": 3, "output": 5, "cacheRead": 0, "cacheWrite": 4622, "totalTokens": 4630,
  "cost": { "input": 0.000015, "output": 0.000125, "total": 0.029 } }
```

### 11. Session State ✅
- `session.isStreaming` — boolean, true during generation
- `session.state` — state object
- `session.messages` — full conversation history

### 12. Session Management ✅
- `session.newSession()` — context restart programmatically
- `session.compact()` — manual compaction
- `SessionManager.inMemory()` — no filesystem persistence needed
- `SessionManager.create(cwd)` — persistent sessions available too

### 13. Stuck Agent Detection ✅
Achievable by monitoring:
- Time since last `text_delta` event while `isStreaming === true`
- Turn duration (`turn_start` to `turn_end`)
- Tool execution duration (`tool_execution_start` to `tool_execution_end`)
- No `agent_end` received after prolonged period
- `session.abort()` available to kill a stuck agent

### 14. Settings Control ✅
`SettingsManager.inMemory({...})` — full control over compaction, retry, etc. per agent.

### Architecture Conclusion
**The SDK is the clear winner for agent runtime.** It provides:
- Full programmatic control (no tmux/RPC needed for agents)
- Real-time observability (tokens, context %, cost, turn count)
- Health monitoring (streaming state, token gaps, stuck detection)
- Extension support (load any pi extension into any agent)
- Clean lifecycle (dispose, restart, compact)

**Remaining question:** Manager self-restart — separate spike (zebwque8).

## Additional Findings

### 15. Fully Async / Parallel Agents ✅
`Promise.all([agentA.prompt(...), agentB.prompt(...)])` — multiple agents run truly simultaneously in one Node process. Tested with Alice & Bob exchanging messages concurrently. See `spike-sdk/main-async.ts`.

### 16. Extension Loading (web-search) ✅
`additionalExtensionPaths: ["/path/to/web-search.ts"]` loads real extensions into SDK sessions. Bob used `web_search` tool to answer Alice's question. See `spike-sdk/main-websearch.ts`.

### 17. Full Isolation ✅
Empty `agentDir` + empty `cwd` = zero discovery. No global extensions, skills, prompts, or themes. Only explicitly provided tools/extensions load. See `spike-sdk/inspect-isolation.ts`.

### 18. Selective Extension Loading with Project Access ✅
`cwd` = project root (for `.pi/` database access), `agentDir` = empty dir (blocks global extensions), `additionalExtensionPaths` = explicit list. Tested: PM extension loaded alone, agent called `epic_list` + `issue_list` successfully against real project database. See `spike-sdk/inspect-pm-test.ts`.

### 19. Persistent Sessions / Transcript ✅
`SessionManager.create(cwd)` writes full JSONL transcript to disk. Every message, model change, thinking level change persisted with tree structure (id/parentId). `SessionManager.inMemory()` available for no-persistence mode. See `spike-sdk/inspect-sessions.ts`.

issues.title: Spike: Manager Extension ↔ Agent Service communication protocol
issues.description:
The architecture is now: **manager pi session** (runs in tmux, has an extension with tools) talks to an **agent service** (TypeScript process using SDK to manage agent sessions). We need to spike the communication layer.

## Key Questions

### 1. Protocol
- WebSocket? Unix socket? HTTP? Filesystem events? stdin/stdout pipe?
- What gives us real-time bidirectional streaming + simplicity?
- Can the manager extension spawn and connect to the service?

### 2. Manager → Service (commands)
- `spawn_agent(name, config)` — start an agent
- `send_message(agent, content)` — deliver a message to an agent
- `get_status(agent)` — context %, tokens, streaming state
- `stop_agent(name)` — kill an agent
- `restart_agent(name)` — context reset

### 3. Service → Manager (events)
- Agent status changes (idle, streaming, stuck)
- Messages FROM agents TO manager (the inbox)
- Token streaming / output tailing
- Context cutoff warnings
- Agent completion / error events

### 4. Message Flow
- How do agents send messages? Custom tool in their session that calls back to the service?
- How does manager receive messages? Events pushed via the protocol?
- Filesystem mailbox still for persistence / audit trail?
- Service as the message router (not direct agent-to-agent filesystem)?

### 5. Future-proofing
- Manager may later become an agent inside the service too
- Protocol should work for both external client (pi extension) and internal (agent-to-agent)

## Deliverable
A working mini-system:
1. A small TypeScript service that manages 2 SDK agent sessions
2. A pi extension that connects to the service and exposes tools
3. Agents exchange messages through the service
4. Manager sees real-time status and message flow

research.content:
## Protocol Decision: Unix Socket

**Why Unix socket:**
- No port conflicts (file-based, e.g. `/tmp/pi-agents-<project>.sock`)
- Low latency, bidirectional streaming
- Natural cleanup (delete file on exit)
- Works perfectly for local-only service

**Queue/buffer needs:**
- Manager may be mid-turn when agent messages arrive → buffer in service, deliver when polled
- Multiple agents may fire events simultaneously → service serializes
- Manager extension polls or gets notified via the socket connection

**Protocol format:** JSON lines over Unix socket (same pattern as RPC mode — proven to work)

**Message flow design:**
1. Service listens on Unix socket
2. Manager extension connects on load
3. Extension sends commands (spawn, message, status)
4. Service pushes events (agent messages, status changes, errors)
5. Extension injects received messages into manager's conversation via `pi.sendUserMessage()` or similar

## Spike Result: Unix Socket Protocol WORKS ✅

Built and tested a complete mini-system in `spike-service/`:

### Components
1. **`protocol.ts`** — Shared types (Command/ServiceEvent), JSONL encode/parse helpers, socket path
2. **`service.ts`** — Agent service: listens on Unix socket, manages SDK sessions, routes messages
3. **`extension/index.ts`** — Pi extension: connects to service, registers tools (agent_spawn, agent_message, agent_status, agent_stop, agent_tail)
4. **`test-client.ts`** — Standalone test client proving the protocol

### Proven Flow
1. ✅ Service listens on `/tmp/pi-agent-service.sock`
2. ✅ Client connects, receives `service_ready`
3. ✅ `spawn_agent` → service creates SDK session → returns `agent_spawned`
4. ✅ `send_message` → service routes to agent via `session.prompt()` or `session.followUp()`
5. ✅ Agent uses `send_message` tool → service detects target is "manager" → pushes `agent_message` event to client
6. ✅ `get_status` → returns per-agent context %, tokens, cost, streaming state, message counts
7. ✅ Extension uses `pi.sendUserMessage()` to inject agent messages into manager conversation

### Key Decisions Validated
- **JSONL over Unix socket** — simple, fast, no dependencies
- **Service as message router** — agents don't talk directly, service mediates
- **Filesystem log for persistence** — `messages.log` as audit trail
- **`prompt()` vs `followUp()`** — use `prompt()` when agent is idle, `followUp()` when streaming

### Minor Issues Found
- `knownAgents` list built at spawn time — needs to be dynamic (agent spawned later not in list)
- Need to handle agent-to-agent messaging (currently only agent→manager tested)

issues.title: Spike: Message Queue, Service Lifecycle & Agent State Design
issues.description:
Design and test the message queue system, service lifecycle management, and agent state tracking. This is a research + prototype spike.

## Questions to Answer

### Service Lifecycle
- How does the manager extension start the service? (spawn child process?)
- How to ensure only ONE service instance runs? (PID file? socket lock?)
- Service must be independent of manager — survives manager restart
- Manager reconnects to existing service on restart
- How to detect if service is already running?

### Message Queue
- Messages persist in the service (not just pass-through)
- Queue per agent — delivered when agent is ready (idle)
- Message states: queued → delivered → read → replied
- Delivery modes:
  - `persist` (default) — queued if offline, delivered when available
  - `online-only` — dropped if agent is offline, but still traced/logged
- ALL messages stored for debugging/audit (even non-queued ones)

### Threads
- Messages belong to threads
- Thread = conversation context between two parties
- Max ~100 messages per thread → auto-instruction to start new thread (loop prevention)
- Thread metadata: participants, message count, created_at, last_activity
- Reply-to support — message references parent message ID

### Message Priority
- `normal` — standard queue order
- `important` — flagged, may interrupt agent (steer vs followUp)
- Priority affects delivery order in queue

### Agent States
- `offline` — not spawned
- `online-idle` — spawned, waiting for work
- `online-working` — actively streaming/processing
- `online-blocked` — waiting for human input or external resource
- State transitions tracked and logged

### Trace & Audit
- Every message logged regardless of delivery mode
- Every state change logged
- Every tool call logged
- Queryable history per agent, per thread, per time range

## Deliverable
- Design document with data structures
- Working prototype testing: queue persistence, thread management, state tracking
- Test: manager restarts, reconnects, picks up queued messages

research.content:
## Existing Messaging System Analysis

### What works well (keep)
- **Thread model** with max 100 messages + loop prevention
- **ACL** — who can talk to who
- **Message format** — frontmatter (from, to, type, timestamp, subject, thread_id) + body
- **Archive on read** — messages move from inbox to archive
- **steer vs followup** delivery modes based on `important` flag
- **File locking** for thread state (prevents race conditions)

### What changes with SDK service architecture
- **No filesystem polling** — service routes messages directly via `session.prompt()`/`session.followUp()`
- **No per-agent inbox dirs** — service holds message queues in memory + persists to single store
- **No file-based message format** — messages are objects in the service, persisted as JSONL
- **ACL moves to settings.json** — single config source
- **Thread tracker is in-service** — no lockfile needed (single process)

### Key patterns to port
1. Thread ID generation and tracking
2. Max 100 messages per thread
3. steer (important) vs followup (normal) delivery
4. Message read/archive tracking
5. Agent brief/description in ACL

## Service Lifecycle Design

### Startup Pattern
1. Manager extension checks if service is running (try connect to socket)
2. If not running → spawn as detached child process (`child_process.spawn` with `detached: true, stdio: 'ignore'`)
3. Service writes PID to `/tmp/pi-agent-service.pid` + locks the socket path
4. Manager extension retries connection until service is ready

### Singleton Guarantee
- Socket path acts as lock — `net.createServer().listen(path)` fails if already bound
- PID file for stale detection — if PID file exists but process is dead, clean up and start fresh
- On startup: check PID file → check if process alive → if dead, remove socket + pid → start

### Manager Reconnect
- Service is independent of manager lifecycle
- On manager restart: extension reconnects to existing service
- Service keeps all state (agents, queues, threads) in memory + persisted to disk
- On reconnect: manager gets `service_ready` + can query state of all agents + any queued messages for "manager"

### Service Shutdown
- Graceful: SIGTERM → dispose all agents → close socket → remove PID file
- Crash: PID file left behind → next startup detects stale, cleans up
- Optional: service auto-shutdown after N minutes of no connected clients

### Service State Persistence
- `{agentsDir}/service-state.jsonl` — append-only log of:
  - Agent spawns/stops
  - All messages (with delivery status)
  - State transitions
  - Thread metadata
- On service restart: replay log to rebuild state

## Message Queue Design

### Message Object
```typescript
interface Message {
  id: string;              // unique ID (uuid or timestamp-based)
  threadId: string;        // thread this belongs to
  from: string;            // sender name
  to: string;              // recipient name
  subject: string;         // short topic (5-10 words)
  body: string;            // markdown content
  priority: "normal" | "important";
  delivery: "persist" | "online-only";
  replyTo?: string;        // parent message ID (for threading)
  timestamp: number;       // created at
  status: "queued" | "delivered" | "read" | "failed";
  deliveredAt?: number;
  readAt?: number;
}
```

### Queue Behavior
- Each agent has a queue (FIFO, priority-aware)
- `important` messages jump to front of queue
- Queue processes when agent is `online-idle`:
  - Pop message → deliver via `session.prompt()` or `session.steer()`
  - Mark as `delivered`
  - Agent's `send_message` tool response marks originating message as `read`
- `online-only` messages: if agent is offline → mark `failed`, still logged
- `persist` messages: stay in queue until agent comes online

### Delivery Strategy
| Priority | Agent State | Action |
|----------|------------|--------|
| normal | idle | `session.prompt(msg)` |
| normal | working | queue, wait for idle |
| important | idle | `session.prompt(msg)` |
| important | working | `session.steer(msg)` — interrupts after current tool |
| any | offline | queue (persist) or fail (online-only) |

### Manager Queue
- Manager is special — messages TO manager are pushed via socket events
- If manager is disconnected: messages queue in service
- On reconnect: service flushes queued manager messages

### Thread Rules
- Thread auto-created on first message without `replyTo`
- Thread ID = `{from}-{timestamp}` or explicit
- Max 100 messages per thread → message rejected with instruction to start new thread
- Thread metadata: participants, message count, created, last_activity

## Spike Result: All Core Patterns Proven ✅

Built and tested `spike-queue/` — transport-agnostic AgentManager with:
- **MessageQueue**: priority, threading, delivery tracking, online-only mode, thread exhaustion at 100
- **AgentManager**: state machine (offline/idle/working), health detection, queue delivery loop, event subscriptions
- **Trace**: append-only JSONL audit log — 129 entries in test covering messages, state changes, deliveries, threads
- All tests passed: queue delivery, agent-to-agent routing, online-only failure, thread exhaustion, state transitions

autoValidation.strategy: User confirms the queue design and service lifecycle patterns answer the requirements

issues.closeMessage: Spike complete. All core patterns proven. Moving to requirements mapping.

validations.criterion:
Design and test the message queue system, service lifecycle management, and agent state tracking. This is a research + prototype spike.

## Questions to Answer

### Service Lifecycle
- How does the manager extension start the service? (spawn child process?)
- How to ensure only ONE service instance runs? (PID file? socket lock?)
- Service must be independent of manager — survives manager restart
- Manager reconnects to existing service on restart
- How to detect if service is already running?

### Message Queue
- Messages persist in the service (not just pass-through)
- Queue per agent — delivered when agent is ready (idle)
- Message states: queued → delivered → read → replied
- Delivery modes:
  - `persist` (default) — queued if offline, delivered when available
  - `online-only` — dropped if agent is offline, but still traced/logged
- ALL messages stored for debugging/audit (even non-queued ones)

### Threads
- Messages belong to threads
- Thread = conversation context between two parties
- Max ~100 messages per thread → auto-instruction to start new thread (loop prevention)
- Thread metadata: participants, message count, created_at, last_activity
- Reply-to support — message references parent message ID

### Message Priority
- `normal` — standard queue order
- `important` — flagged, may interrupt agent (steer vs followUp)
- Priority affects delivery order in queue

### Agent States
- `offline` — not spawned
- `online-idle` — spawned, waiting for work
- `online-working` — actively streaming/processing
- `online-blocked` — waiting for human input or external resource
- State transitions tracked and logged

### Trace & Audit
- Every message logged regardless of delivery mode
- Every state change logged
- Every tool call logged
- Queryable history per agent, per thread, per time range

## Deliverable
- Design document with data structures
- Working prototype testing: queue persistence, thread management, state tracking
- Test: manager restarts, reconnects, picks up queued messages
validations.evidence: User confirmed "Yes its solid". All patterns proven: queue, threading, state machine, trace, health monitoring. Requirements asset created as project-level reference.

issues.title: Spike: Service lifecycle — spawn, survive, reconnect
issues.description:
Test the critical service lifecycle flows:

## Service Start/Stop/Reconnect
1. **Spawn from extension** — can a pi extension spawn a detached Node process that survives?
2. **Singleton** — socket lock prevents duplicate services
3. **Survive manager exit** — kill pi, service keeps running, agents keep working
4. **Reconnect** — restart pi, extension connects to existing service, gets queued messages
5. **Graceful shutdown** — SIGTERM cleans up agents, socket, PID file
6. **Stale detection** — if service crashed, detect stale PID/socket and restart

## Agent Context Cutoff & Handoff
7. **Context monitoring** — service watches `getContextUsage().percent` per agent
8. **Steer interrupt** — at threshold, `session.steer()` forces agent to stop current work
9. **Handoff tool** — agent calls `context_handoff(summary, continueMessage)` to save state
10. **Session reset** — service calls `session.newSession()` on the same session object
11. **Continue** — service prompts with continueMessage + system prompt after reset
12. **Does newSession() keep the session object alive?** Or do we need to create a fresh one?

## Deliverables
- Working service spawn/reconnect test
- Working context cutoff → handoff → reset → continue test

research.content:
## Research: Detached Process Spawning in Node.js

Standard pattern for spawning a service that survives parent:
```typescript
const child = spawn('node', ['service.js'], {
  detached: true,
  stdio: 'ignore',
  env: { ...process.env },
});
child.unref(); // parent won't wait for child
```

For tsx (TypeScript): `spawn('npx', ['tsx', 'service.ts'], { detached: true, stdio: 'ignore' })`

## Research: session.newSession()
From SDK docs: `newSession(options?: { parentSession?: string }): Promise<boolean>` — returns boolean indicating success. Need to test if the session object remains usable after this call.

## All Tests Passed ✅

### Service Lifecycle (spike-lifecycle/test-lifecycle.ts)
- Detached spawn with `child.unref()` — service independent of parent
- Socket lock = singleton guarantee
- Service survives parent exit, queues messages while disconnected
- Reconnect flushes queued messages (3 messages delivered)
- SIGTERM graceful cleanup (socket + PID file removed)
- Stale PID detection via `process.kill(pid, 0)`

### Context Cutoff Handoff (spike-lifecycle/test-cutoff2.ts)
- Agent calls `context_handoff(summary, continueMessage)` on instruction
- `session.newSession()` returns true, resets to 0 messages/0 tokens
- Session object stays alive and usable after reset
- Agent continues with handoff message in fresh context
- Full cycle: 5753 tokens → 0 → 5494 tokens

## System Prompt Finding

- `systemPromptOverride` **fully replaces** pi's default system prompt (only override text + date + cwd remain)
- Without override, pi injects: coding guidelines, tool listing, doc references (~1553 chars)
- **Tool schemas are sent via the API**, NOT in the system prompt — the "Available tools:" section is redundant for Anthropic
- `before_agent_start` event can **append** to existing prompt instead of replacing
- **Decision:** Use override with complete agent prompt (SYSTEM.md + framework instructions). Include relevant pi guidelines in our own templates. No dependency on pi's internal prompt format.

autoValidation.strategy: User confirms service survives pi restart and reconnect works

issues.closeMessage: Lifecycle spike complete. All patterns proven and documented as assets.

5. **Graceful shutdown** — SIGTERM cleans up agents, socket, PID file
6. **Stale detection** — if service crashed, detect stale PID/socket and restart

## Agent Context Cutoff & Handoff

issues.title: Define Manager Extension Tool Inventory
issues.description:
Map all tools the manager extension registers, with complete parameter schemas and response formats.

These are the tools the human operator (or future manager agent) uses to control the agent service.

Categories:
1. **Agent management** — spawn, stop, restart, list
2. **Messaging** — send message, view all messages, view threads
3. **Observability** — status, tail, peek, dashboard
4. **Service** — start service, stop service, service status
5. **Configuration** — reload config, view config

For each tool: name, description, parameters (TypeBox schema), response format, example usage.

research.content:
## Manager Extension Tool Inventory

### 1. Agent Management

#### `agent_spawn`
Spawn a new agent session in the service.
```
Parameters:
  name: string          — unique agent identifier
  config_override?: {   — optional overrides (defaults from settings.json)
    model?: string
    thinking?: string
    extensions?: string[]
    cutoff_polite?: number
    cutoff_hard?: number
  }
Response: "Agent {name} spawned" or error
```
Notes: reads `.pi/agents/{name}/SYSTEM.md` for system prompt, merges with settings.json config.

#### `agent_stop`
Stop a running agent.
```
Parameters:
  name: string
Response: "Agent {name} stopped"
```

#### `agent_restart`
Context reset — triggers handoff + newSession.
```
Parameters:
  name: string
  continue_message?: string  — optional override (otherwise uses agent's default init prompt)
Response: "Agent {name} restarted with fresh context"
```

#### `agent_list`
List all agents with current state.
```
Parameters: (none)
Response: table of agents with state, context %, health, uptime
```

---

### 2. Messaging

#### `agent_message`
Send a message to an agent.
```
Parameters:
  to: string              — agent name
  subject: string         — short topic (5-10 words)
  message: string         — body content (markdown)
  important?: boolean     — priority flag (default: false)
  thread_id?: string      — continue existing thread (omit for new)
Response: "Message sent to {to} [thread: {id}, #{count}]"
```

#### `agent_messages`
View messages — all, per-agent, or per-thread.
```
Parameters:
  agent?: string          — filter by agent (sent or received)
  thread_id?: string      — filter by thread
  limit?: number          — max messages (default: 20)
  include_agent_to_agent?: boolean  — include inter-agent msgs (default: true)
Response: formatted message list with from, to, subject, status, timestamp
```

#### `agent_threads`
List active threads.
```
Parameters:
  agent?: string          — filter by participant
  limit?: number          — default: 10
Response: thread list with id, subject, participants, message count, last activity
```

---

### 3. Observability

#### `agent_status`
Get status of all agents or one agent.
```
Parameters:
  name?: string           — specific agent (omit for all)
Response: per-agent: state, context %, tokens, cost, messages sent/recv/queued, health, uptime
```

#### `agent_tail`
Enable/disable real-time output streaming for an agent.
```
Parameters:
  name: string
  enabled: boolean
Response: "Tailing enabled/disabled for {name}"
```
Note: text deltas injected as status updates or logged.

#### `agent_peek`
Get last N lines of agent output (recent conversation).
```
Parameters:
  name: string
  lines?: number          — default: 20
Response: last N assistant messages or text fragments
```

#### `agent_history`
Get full agent conversation history.
```
Parameters:
  name: string
  limit?: number          — default: 50
Response: full message history (user + assistant turns)
```

---

### 4. Service Management

#### `agent_service_start`
Start the agent service (if not running).
```
Parameters: (none)
Response: "Service started (PID: {pid})" or "Service already running (PID: {pid})"
```
Note: usually auto-started, this is for manual control.

#### `agent_service_stop`
Stop the agent service (stops all agents).
```
Parameters:
  force?: boolean         — kill without graceful shutdown (default: false)
Response: "Service stopped. {N} agents disposed."
```

#### `agent_service_status`
Get service health info.
```
Parameters: (none)
Response: PID, uptime, agent count, total cost, total messages, queue depths
```

---

### 5. Trace / Debug

#### `agent_trace`
Query the trace log.
```
Parameters:
  agent?: string          — filter by agent
  type?: string           — filter by entry type (message, state_change, delivery, etc.)
  last?: number           — last N entries (default: 20)
Response: formatted trace entries
```

## Agent-Side Tools (registered by service per agent)

#### `send_message`
Send a message to another agent or manager.
```
Parameters:
  to: string, subject: string, message: string,
  important?: boolean, thread_id?: string
```

#### `context_handoff`
Save state for context reset.
```
Parameters:
  summary: string, continueMessage: string
```

#### `list_messages`
Query own sent/received message history.
```
Parameters:
  thread_id?: string      — filter by thread
  limit?: number          — default: 10
Response: recent messages with from, to, subject, body preview, timestamp
```

#### `list_threads`
See own active threads.
```
Parameters: (none)
Response: threads the agent participates in, with message counts and last activity
```

### On Session Start / Context Reset
Service injects a message overview into the agent's first prompt:
```
## 📬 Message Overview
- 3 unread messages waiting
- Active threads:
  - "Feature X implementation" with manager (12 msgs, last: 2min ago)
  - "Code review feedback" with reviewer (5 msgs, last: 10min ago)
- Last context reset: 5 min ago
- Handoff summary: {summary from previous session}
```

This matches the existing pattern from core/shared/messaging.ts startup injection.

## Manager Monitor/Loop Tools

#### `manager_monitor`
Periodic oversight loop — the manager runs unattended doing cycles of check/act/sleep.
```
Parameters:
  action: "start" | "next" | "stop"

start → resets turn counter, loads MONITOR.md instructions + FINDINGS.md, begins loop
next  → sleeps configured interval (countdown widget), then injects next cycle prompt
stop  → stops loop, resets counter

Config: interval_seconds, max_turns (from settings.json)
```
State persists to file so it survives context resets.

#### `manager_defer`
Store an unsolvable issue for follow-up (agent is running unattended, can't ask human).
```
Parameters:
  title: string           — short description
  details: string         — full context, what was tried, why it's blocked
  priority?: "low" | "medium" | "high"
Response: "Deferred issue #{N} stored. Will be presented on next human interaction."
```
Stored in `.pi/agents/deferred.jsonl` — presented to human when they next interact.

#### `manager_loop_end`
Signal that unattended work for this cycle is complete. Triggers the countdown/wait.
```
Parameters:
  summary: string         — what was accomplished this cycle
Response: triggers countdown, then next cycle starts
```

### Session Start Warnings

On extension load (`session_start`):
- **Check TMUX**: if `process.env.TMUX` is not set, inject warning:
  ```
  ⚠️ WARNING: Pi is NOT running in tmux. Context restart is NOT possible.
  The manager CANNOT self-restart on context cutoff. Start pi inside tmux for full functionality.
  ```
- **Check service**: if agent service not running, inject info about starting it

### Context Cutoff (Manager)
- Manager extension monitors its own context via `before_agent_start`
- At polite threshold: inject "Wrap up and prepare for context restart"
- At hard threshold: inject "STOP. Prepare context restart NOW."
- Manager calls `/new` via tmux injection to restart (requires tmux)
- Monitor state persists across restart via `monitor-state.json`

## Status Bar

Mode always visible via `ctx.ui.setStatus()`:

```
🏗️ SETUP | 0 agents | service: running
🟢 OPERATE | 3 agents (2 idle, 1 working) | service: running  
🔄 LOOP 4/10 | 3 agents | ⏱️ 7:23 | 2 subscriptions | service: running
🔧 CONFIGURE | 3 agents | service: running
🐛 DEBUG | 3 agents | service: running
```

In loop mode also shows: cycle count, countdown timer, active subscription count.

Implementation: `pi.on("agent_end")` + polling interval updates the status text. Mode icon changes with `manager_mode` tool call.

## Default Subscriptions

Two subscriptions are **always active by default** on every session start:
- `agent_message` — messages arriving for manager
- `agent_state_change` — agent goes stuck/error/stopped

The manager can temporarily mute them in the current session:
```
manager_unsubscribe type:agent_message    → muted for this session
manager_unsubscribe type:agent_state_change → muted for this session
```

But on context restart / new session, they come back. These are NOT persisted as "disabled" — they're hardcoded defaults that reset.

Custom subscriptions (file_modified, log_error, etc.) are the opposite — they persist across restarts and must be explicitly removed.

## System Prompt Assembly (hardcoded, automatic)

### Agent system prompt (built by service):
```
1. Agent's SYSTEM.md              ← user-written, from .pi/agents/<name>/SYSTEM.md
2. Coding guidelines              ← hardcoded, auto-included if agent has coding tools
3. Unattended agent instructions  ← HARDCODED in service, always appended
4. Message overview               ← auto-generated on start/reset (threads, unread count)
5. Handoff summary                ← auto-injected after context reset (from previous session)
```

### Manager system prompt (built by extension):
```
1. Manager's SYSTEM.md            ← from .pi/agents/manager/SYSTEM.md
2. Mode instructions              ← HARDCODED per mode, auto-injected on mode switch
3. Monitor state                  ← auto-injected if loop is active
4. Message overview               ← auto-generated on start/reset
```

**None of the hardcoded parts are editable by the user.** They're framework internals. The user only writes SYSTEM.md files.

issues.closeMessage: Complete. 52 tools mapped across 5 modes. Reusable specs in 3 assets.

issues.title: Design settings.json schema
issues.description:
Design the complete `.pi/agents/settings.json` schema. This is the single source of configuration for the entire agent framework.

Must cover:
- Global defaults (provider, model, thinking level)
- ACL (who can message who, agent briefs)
- Per-agent config (model overrides, extensions, cutoff thresholds, spawn settings)
- Monitor/loop settings (interval, max_turns)
- Service settings (socket path, log level)

Deliverable: A complete JSON schema as an asset, with an example settings.json.

research.content:
## Inputs for Schema Design

Sources to pull from:
- Existing `config/acl.json` — current ACL format
- Existing `config/agents/worker/agent.json` — current per-agent config
- Requirements asset (1hfs3f9f) — configuration section
- Tool inventory asset (5c5nibh6) — mode/tool mappings reference
- Spike findings — what settings the SDK needs (model, thinking, extensions, compaction disabled)

## No Path Leaking Rule

**CRITICAL:** No tool response or system prompt injection should ever contain filesystem paths. All agent references use `name` only.

- ✅ `agent_config_show name:worker` → shows config fields, NOT the folder path
- ✅ `agent_list` → shows names + status, NOT paths
- ✅ `agent_system_prompt_show name:worker` → shows content, NOT file path
- ✅ `agent_trace` → shows events by agent name, NOT file locations
- ✅ Extension names shown as short names ("project-management"), NOT resolved paths

The service resolves names → paths internally. Tools only accept and return names.

## Gap Fix: agent_create / agent_delete

`agent_create` must:
1. Create folder + agent.json + SYSTEM.md
2. Add path to settings.json `agents[]` array

`agent_delete` must:
1. Remove path from settings.json `agents[]` array
2. Optionally remove folder (with confirmation)

Both tools accept a `name` only — the service determines where to create/find the folder (defaults to `.pi/agents/{name}/`).

## agent_register / agent_unregister

#### `agent_register`
Register an agent. Creates folder + files if needed, or registers an existing folder.
```
Parameters:
  name: string              — agent name (for new agents)
  path?: string             — optional folder path (default: .pi/agents/{name}/)
                               use absolute path for agents outside the repo
Response: "Agent {name} registered"
```
- If path exists with agent.json → registers it as-is
- If path doesn't exist → creates folder + agent.json + SYSTEM.md skeleton

#### `agent_unregister`
Remove agent from settings.json. Does NOT delete the folder.
```
Parameters:
  name: string
Response: "Agent {name} unregistered (folder preserved)"
```

Replaces `agent_create` / `agent_delete` in the tool inventory.

## Debug Mode Path Visibility

In DEBUG mode only, tools reveal internal details:
- `agent_inspect` shows: folder path, resolved extension paths, socket path, PID
- `agent_list` in debug adds a "path" column
- `config_show` shows full settings.json including paths

In all other modes, paths are hidden — only names shown.

## agent_register Scaffold Behavior

When registering and the folder/agent.json doesn't exist, the tool:

1. Creates the folder structure
2. Scaffolds agent.json + SYSTEM.md + AGENTS.md
3. Returns the scaffolded content + clear instructions

**Response on new agent scaffold:**
```
Agent "researcher" registered and scaffolded.

## Created: agent.json
{
  "name": "researcher",
  "brief": "TODO: Describe what this agent does in 1-2 sentences.",
  "extensions": [],
  "coding_tools": true,
  "auto_spawn": false
}

## Created: SYSTEM.md
# Agent: researcher

TODO: Define this agent's role, responsibilities, and behavior.

## Created: AGENTS.md
(empty — agent will write learnings here)

## Next Steps
1. Edit agent.json — set the `brief` (other agents see this)
2. Edit SYSTEM.md — define the agent's role and instructions
3. Configure ACL — add messaging permissions in settings
4. Optionally set model/provider/extensions overrides in agent.json
5. Use `agent_spawn` to start the agent when ready
```

This way the manager (or user in setup mode) sees exactly what was created and knows what to do next.

issues.description:

4. Trace entry types and query API
5. Event subscription model with bounded delivery

research.content:
Key inputs from existing requirements and spikes:

**Service API** — from requirements asset:
- Uses SDK `createAgentSession` for all agents
- Transport-agnostic core — protocol adapters wrap it
- State recovery from trace.jsonl replay
- Singleton per project

**Protocol** — from spike 44uom9qq:
- Unix socket with newline-delimited JSON
- Request/response with `id` field + server-pushed events
- Manager extension is the client

**State Machine** — from requirements:
- States: offline → online-idle → online-working (→ online-blocked future)
- Health: healthy / slow / stuck via token gap timing
- Context cutoff: polite (70%) and hard (90%) thresholds

**Trace** — from requirements:
- Append-only JSONL
- Every message, state transition, spawn/stop, tool call
- Queryable by agent, thread, time range, type
- Replayable for state recovery

**Events** — from requirements:
- Bounded subscriptions (max N then auto-unsubscribe)
- Filters: event type, agent name, thread
- Prevents infinite loops

**⚠️ VALIDATION STATUS: These 5 requirement assets are NOT yet validated by the user.** They are reference designs that should be followed as the default plan, but can be deviated from during implementation if practical concerns arise. They are not hard constraints — treat them as strong guidance that can be broken with justification.

issues.title: Agent state machine, health monitor, and cutoff
issues.description:
Implement `src/health.ts` and `src/cutoff.ts`:

**State machine** (in ManagedAgent):
- States: offline → online-idle → online-working, with transitions per [ik1yp2tc]

**Health monitor** (`HealthMonitor`):
- Per-agent 5s interval timer, started on agent_start, stopped on agent_end
- Token gap detection: healthy (<10s), slow (10-60s), stuck (>60s or no events 120s)

- Checks `session.getContextUsage().percent` on agent_end
- Polite cutoff: enqueues warning message when threshold hit
- Hard cutoff: sets `hardSteering` flag, steers on `tool_execution_end`
- Reset on context_handoff

Dependencies: TraceWriter, SettingsLoader (for thresholds).

- `manager.shutdown()` → stop all agents, close trace, remove socket + PID file
- Trace `service_stopped` entry

assets.title: SDK Basic: Create Two Agents with Different Personalities
assets.context: When creating agent sessions with the SDK — shows the fundamental pattern for spawning agents with custom system prompts, custom tools, and in-memory sessions
assets.body:
## Pattern: Multiple Agent Sessions

Create multiple `AgentSession` instances in one Node process, each with unique system prompts and tools.

```typescript
const { session } = await createAgentSession({
  model,
  thinkingLevel: "off",
  authStorage, modelRegistry,
  tools: [],                          // no built-in coding tools
  customTools: makeTools(name),       // per-agent custom tools
  sessionManager: SessionManager.inMemory(),
  settingsManager: SettingsManager.inMemory({ compaction: { enabled: false } }),
  systemPromptOverride: "You are Alice, a cheerful programmer...",
});
```

### Key Points
- Each session is fully independent — no shared state
- `tools: []` removes all built-in tools (read, bash, edit, write)
- `customTools` provides per-agent tool sets
- `systemPromptOverride` sets the agent's personality/role
- `SessionManager.inMemory()` = no filesystem persistence
- `SettingsManager.inMemory()` = no global settings

### Spike Code
`spike-sdk/main.ts` — Alice (cheerful) and Bob (grumpy) exchange 5 messages via filesystem mailbox. Sequential turn-taking orchestrated by the main process.

sources.description: Two agents exchanging messages sequentially with custom system prompts and filesystem mailbox

assets.title: SDK Async: Fully Parallel Agent Execution
assets.context: When running multiple agents simultaneously — shows that Promise.all works for concurrent agent prompting with no deadlocks
assets.body:
## Pattern: Async Parallel Agents

Run multiple agents truly simultaneously via `Promise.all`. Each agent has its own async loop.

```typescript
const results = await Promise.all([
  runAgentLoop(alice, "alice", true),   // starts conversation
  runAgentLoop(bob, "bob", false),      // polls for messages
]);
```

Each loop:
1. Check messages (filesystem mailbox)
2. If message found, reply
3. Wait, repeat

### Key Points
- No deadlocks — filesystem mailbox handles concurrent read/write
- Agents interleave naturally based on LLM response times
- Timing matters — polling delay affects message sync (use event-driven in production)
- Multiple `session.prompt()` calls can run concurrently across sessions

### Spike Code
`spike-sdk/main-async.ts` — Both agents run simultaneously, 5 message exchanges, ~66s with Haiku.

sources.description: Two agents running fully async via Promise.all with concurrent mailbox polling

assets.title: SDK Isolation: Fully Controlled Agent Environment
assets.context: When agents must be isolated from global/project extensions — shows how to create a blank-slate agent with only explicit tools and extensions
assets.body:
## Pattern: Full Agent Isolation

Block all global and project discovery by pointing dirs at empty locations.

```typescript
const emptyAgentDir = mkdtempSync(join(tmpdir(), "pi-agent-isolated-"));
const emptyCwd = mkdtempSync(join(tmpdir(), "pi-agent-cwd-"));

const loader = new DefaultResourceLoader({
  cwd: emptyCwd,              // no project extensions
  agentDir: emptyAgentDir,    // no global extensions
  settingsManager: SettingsManager.inMemory({ ... }),
  systemPromptOverride: () => "Custom prompt.",
});
```

### Result
- 0 extensions, 0 skills, 0 prompts, 0 themes loaded
- Only explicitly provided `customTools` and `additionalExtensionPaths` are available
- System prompt is just the override + date/cwd (127 chars)
- Auth still uses `AuthStorage.create()` for API keys (can override path)

### Spike Code
`spike-sdk/inspect-isolation.ts` — Agent created with only a custom `ping` tool, nothing else.

sources.description: Fully isolated agent with zero discovery — only custom ping tool

assets.title: SDK Selective Access: Project Extensions Without Global Leaks
assets.context: When agents need project database access (e.g., PM tools) but must not load global extensions — shows the cwd + empty agentDir + explicit extensions pattern
assets.body:
## Pattern: Project Access Without Global Leaks

Give agents access to `.pi/` database while blocking all global extensions.

```typescript
const loader = new DefaultResourceLoader({
  cwd: projectRoot,           // real project — agent can access .pi/ database
  agentDir: emptyDir,         // empty — blocks ~/.pi global extensions
  additionalExtensionPaths: [
    "/path/to/pi-extension-project-management/index.ts",
  ],
  settingsManager: SettingsManager.inMemory({ ... }),
});
```

### Result
- PM extension loads and finds `.pi/` database via cwd
- Agent gets all 30 PM tools (epic_*, issue_*, asset_*, etc.)
- No other global extensions leak in
- Agent successfully called `epic_list` and `issue_list` against real project data

### Config Pattern for settings.json
```json
{
  "agents": {
    "worker":     { "extensions": ["project-management", "web-search"] },
    "reviewer":   { "extensions": ["project-management"] },
    "researcher": { "extensions": ["web-search"] }
  }
}
```

Framework resolves extension names → paths → `additionalExtensionPaths`.

### Spike Code
`spike-sdk/inspect-pm.ts` — Isolated agent with PM extension only.
`spike-sdk/inspect-pm-test.ts` — Agent calls PM tools and reports project status.

assets.title: SDK Sessions: Persistent Transcripts & In-Memory Mode
assets.context: When choosing session persistence strategy for agents — shows both in-memory and persistent JSONL options
assets.body:
## Pattern: Session Persistence

### In-Memory (no disk)
```typescript
SessionManager.inMemory()
// session.sessionFile → undefined
// session.messages → available in memory, gone after dispose()
```

### Persistent (full JSONL transcript)
```typescript
SessionManager.create(cwd)
// Writes to: ~/.pi/agent/sessions/<cwd-slug>/<timestamp>_<id>.jsonl
```

### What Gets Persisted (JSONL entries)
1. Session metadata — version, id, timestamp, cwd
2. Model changes — provider, model id
3. Thinking level changes
4. Every message — user and assistant, with full content
5. Tree structure — `id`/`parentId` for branching
6. Assistant messages include usage/cost data

### For Agent Framework
- Use `SessionManager.create(agentSessionDir)` per agent
- Store in `.pi/agents/<name>/sessions/` → inspectable, portable
- Same JSONL format as regular pi sessions — can open with `pi` to inspect
- `session.messages` for live transcript access
- `session.newSession()` for context restart (preserves session lineage)

### Spike Code

assets.title: RPC Mode: Headless Agent Control via JSON Protocol
assets.context: When considering process-isolated agents or manager self-restart — reference for RPC mode capabilities vs SDK
assets.body:
## RPC Mode Overview

Run pi as a headless subprocess controlled via JSONL over stdin/stdout.

```bash
pi --mode rpc --no-session --model "anthropic/claude-haiku-3-5"
```

### Commands (send to stdin)
- `prompt` / `steer` / `follow_up` — prompting
- `abort` — kill current operation
- `get_state` / `get_messages` / `get_session_stats` — observability
- `set_model` / `cycle_model` — model control
- `compact` / `new_session` — context management
- `bash` — execute shell commands

### Events (read from stdout)
Same event types as SDK: `agent_start/end`, `turn_start/end`, `message_update` (text_delta), `tool_execution_*`

### SDK vs RPC Comparison
| Capability | SDK | RPC |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Process isolation | ❌ shared | ✅ separate |
| Crash isolation | ❌ shared | ✅ separate |
| Performance | ✅ direct calls | ❌ IPC overhead |
| Simplicity | ✅ method calls | ❌ JSON serialize |
| Extensions | ✅ explicit loading | ✅ auto-discovery |
| Observability | ✅ direct objects | ✅ JSON events |

### When to Use RPC
- Crash isolation needed (one agent crash shouldn't kill others)
- Memory isolation (separate V8 heaps)
- Manager self-restart (spawn new process, kill old one)

### Architecture Decision
SDK is primary for agent runtime. RPC is fallback for manager self-restart or crash-sensitive deployments. Not yet spike-tested with code.

assets.title: Service Architecture: Unix Socket Protocol for Manager ↔ Agent Service
assets.context: When designing the communication between the manager pi extension and the agent service — the proven protocol and message flow pattern
assets.body:
## Architecture: Unix Socket Service

```
Manager (pi + extension)  ←— JSONL/Unix socket —→  Agent Service (Node + SDK)
   │                                                    │
   ├─ agent_spawn tool ──→ spawn_agent cmd ──→ createAgentSession()
   ├─ agent_message tool ──→ send_message cmd ──→ session.prompt()
   ├─ agent_status tool ──→ get_status cmd ──→ getContextUsage() + getSessionStats()
   ├─ agent_stop tool ──→ stop_agent cmd ──→ session.dispose()
   ├─ agent_tail tool ──→ tail_agent cmd ──→ subscribe(text_delta)
   │
   └─ pi.sendUserMessage() ←── agent_message event ←── agent calls send_message tool
```

### Protocol: JSONL over Unix Socket
- Socket path: `/tmp/pi-agent-service.sock`
- One JSON object per line, `\n` delimited
- Bidirectional: commands in, events out

### Commands (Manager → Service)
- `spawn_agent` — name, systemPrompt, extensions[]
- `stop_agent` — name
- `send_message` — from, to, content (service routes)
- `get_status` — returns all agent stats
- `tail_agent` — enable/disable text delta streaming

### Events (Service → Manager)
- `agent_spawned` / `agent_stopped`
- `agent_message` — message routed to manager
- `all_status` — per-agent context %, tokens, cost, streaming state
- `agent_tail` — text deltas for real-time output
- `agent_error` — error notifications

### Message Routing
- Service is the central router — agents don't talk directly
- Agents have a `send_message` tool that calls back to the service
- Service checks if target is "manager" → push event, or another agent → `session.prompt()`
- All messages persisted to `messages.log` for audit trail
- Use `prompt()` when agent idle, `followUp()` when streaming

### Extension Integration
- Extension connects on `session_start`, reconnects on disconnect
- `pi.sendUserMessage()` injects agent messages into manager conversation
- Tools are simple command senders with response waiters

### Spike Code
- `spike-service/protocol.ts` — shared types and JSONL helpers
- `spike-service/service.ts` — the agent service
- `spike-service/extension/index.ts` — the manager extension
- `spike-service/test-client.ts` — standalone protocol test

sources.description: Shared protocol types, JSONL helpers, socket path
Agent service: SDK session management, Unix socket server, message routing
Manager extension: connects to service, registers agent tools, injects messages
Standalone test client proving the full protocol flow

assets.title: Transport-Agnostic Service API — Critical Constraint
assets.context: When building the agent service — ALL service logic must be transport-agnostic. The protocol layer is a thin adapter.
assets.body:
## CRITICAL: Transport-Agnostic Architecture

The AgentService MUST be a pure TypeScript API class with no transport dependencies. All communication protocols are thin adapters layered on top.

### Core API (no transport)
```typescript
class AgentService {
  spawnAgent(name, config): Promise<void>
  stopAgent(name): Promise<void>
  sendMessage(from, to, content): void
  getStatus(): Record<string, AgentStatus>
  getAgentStatus(name): AgentStatus
  peekAgent(name, lines): string[]
  getAgentHistory(name): Message[]
  subscribe(callback: (event: ServiceEvent) => void): unsubscribe
}
```

### Transport Adapters (thin wrappers)
- **UnixSocketAdapter** — current implementation, JSONL framing
- **WebSocketAdapter** — future, for remote/browser clients
- **RestAdapter** — future, for HTTP API access
- **DirectAdapter** — future, for in-process use (manager as SDK agent)

### Why This Matters
1. Manager extension today uses Unix socket — but later the manager itself may become an agent inside the service (DirectAdapter)
2. Web UI / dashboard will need WebSocket or REST
3. Remote agents or cloud deployment needs network transport
4. Testing is easier with direct API calls

### Rule
**Never put business logic in the transport layer.** The adapter only serializes/deserializes and forwards to the core API.

assets.title: Agent Framework Requirements
assets.context: When designing or implementing any part of the agent framework — the master checklist of all requirements
assets.body:
> **📋 Live document.** Update when implementation drifts from spec or when decisions change. Not all items are validated — treat as strong guidance, not hard constraints.

## Agent Framework — Requirements

### Service Architecture
- [ ] Single agent service process (TypeScript, uses Pi SDK)
- [ ] Manager pi session connects via Unix socket (WebSocket/REST later)
- [ ] Service is independent of manager — survives manager restart
- [ ] Manager reconnects and picks up queued messages on restart
- [ ] Singleton guarantee — only one service instance per project
- [ ] Service auto-started by manager extension if not running
- [ ] Transport-agnostic core API — protocol adapters are thin wrappers
- [ ] All agent sessions managed via SDK `createAgentSession`
- [ ] State recovery on service crash — replay trace.jsonl to rebuild queues/threads
- [ ] systemPromptOverride fully replaces pi default — agent SYSTEM.md + framework instructions

### Agent Lifecycle
- [ ] Agents spawned with custom system prompts, tools, and extensions
- [ ] Agent states: offline → online-idle → online-working → see [ik1yp2tc]
- [ ] All state transitions logged
- [ ] Health monitoring: healthy / slow / stuck detection via token gap timing → see [ik1yp2tc]
- [ ] Agents fully isolated — empty agentDir, explicit extension loading only
- [ ] cwd always project root
- [ ] Agent context restart (`session.newSession()`) and compaction (`session.compact()`)
- [ ] Clean agent stop with `session.dispose()`

### Context Cutoff — Two-Level
- [ ] Service monitors `session.getContextUsage().percent` per agent
- [ ] NEVER interrupt a tool call — always wait for tool to complete
- [ ] **Polite cutoff** (configurable, e.g. 70%) — after current turn completes, queue handoff prompt
- [ ] **Hard cutoff** (configurable, e.g. 90%) — steer between every tool call until agent complies
- [ ] Service captures handoff → `session.newSession()` → prompts with continueMessage
- [ ] Both thresholds configurable per agent in settings.json
- [ ] `context_handoff` tool registered automatically for every agent
- [ ] Full flow documented in [ik1yp2tc]

### Messaging
- [ ] Service is the central message router — no direct agent-to-agent
- [ ] ALL messages traced and persisted (append-only JSONL trace log)
- [ ] Manager can see ALL messages — including agent-to-agent
- [ ] Messages have: id, threadId, from, to, subject, body, priority, delivery mode, status
- [ ] Message statuses: queued → delivered → read → (replied via new message)
- [ ] Delivery modes: `persist` (default, queued if offline) and `online-only` (dropped if offline, still traced)
- [ ] Priority: `normal` (queue order) and `important` (front of queue, can interrupt via steer)
- [ ] Queue per agent — delivered when agent is idle → **triggers a new turn** via `session.prompt()`
- [ ] Manager queue — pushed via socket events, flushed on reconnect

### Event Subscriptions — Manager-Only
- [ ] Manager subscribes to service events via protocol → see [il2m3sl0]
- [ ] Subscriptions are bounded — max N events then auto-unsubscribe
- [ ] Agents do NOT have subscriptions — they are message-driven only
- [ ] Prevents infinite event → reaction → event loops

### Threading
- [ ] Messages belong to threads
- [ ] Thread auto-created on first message
- [ ] Max 100 messages per thread (loop prevention) — rejected with instruction to start new
- [ ] Thread metadata: id, subject, participants, message count, timestamps
- [ ] Reply-to support — message references parent message ID

### Observability
- [ ] Per-agent status: context %, tokens, cost, message stats, health, state
- [ ] Per-agent conversation peek (last N lines of output)
- [ ] Per-agent message history
- [ ] Aggregate dashboard: all agents at a glance
- [ ] Real-time agent tailing → separate epic [p09o4o3f] (user-facing, not manager tool)

### Trace / Audit
- [ ] Append-only JSONL trace log — never modified → see [f5z68c4v]
- [ ] Every message logged (including failed online-only)
- [ ] Every state transition logged
- [ ] Every agent spawn/stop logged
- [ ] Queryable by agent, thread, time range, type
- [ ] Replayable — service can rebuild state from trace on restart

### Configuration
- [ ] Single settings file: `.pi/agents/settings.json` → see [n2j36dl7]
- [ ] Contains: ACL, defaults (provider, model), per-agent paths
- [ ] Per-agent config in agent.json: extensions list, cutoff thresholds, coding_tools flag
- [ ] Per-agent subfolder for SYSTEM.md, AGENTS.md
- [ ] Agent folders are portable — can live anywhere on filesystem

### Manager
- [ ] Runs in tmux (for self-restart on context cutoff)
- [ ] Has extension with mode-based tools → see [5c5nibh6]
- [ ] Receives agent messages via socket events → injected via `pi.sendUserMessage()`
- [ ] Can view all inter-agent messages (full visibility)
- [ ] May later become an agent inside the service (future-proof)

### Extensions & Tools
- [ ] Agents get tools via `customTools` + `additionalExtensionPaths`
- [ ] Per-agent extension config in agent.json
- [ ] Agent-side tools registered by service → see [0l0qkut7]
- [ ] Agents can use project extensions (PM, web-search, etc.) when configured
- [ ] No global extension leakage — only explicitly configured extensions load

### Future (not MVP but design for)
- [ ] WebSocket transport adapter
- [ ] REST transport adapter
- [ ] Manager as agent inside the service (DirectAdapter)
- [ ] Web UI / dashboard
- [ ] Agent-to-agent direct communication (bypassing manager)
- [ ] Session persistence per agent (JSONL transcripts)
- [ ] Multiple projects / multi-tenant

assets.title: Service Core: Transport-Agnostic AgentManager with Queue & State Machine
assets.context: When building the agent service — the proven core API pattern with message queue, state machine, trace, and health monitoring
assets.body:
## Pattern: AgentManager Core API

Transport-agnostic service that manages agent sessions, message queues, and state.

### Components
- **`AgentManager`** — spawns agents, routes messages, tracks state, emits events
- **`MessageQueue`** — per-agent FIFO with priority, threading, delivery tracking
- **`Trace`** — append-only JSONL audit log, replayable

### Key APIs
```typescript
mgr.spawnAgent(name, systemPrompt, extensions?)
mgr.stopAgent(name)
mgr.sendMessage(from, to, subject, body, { priority, delivery, threadId })
mgr.getAllStatus()          // per-agent context %, tokens, cost, health
mgr.subscribe(callback)    // events: state changes, messages, spawns
mgr.queue.listThreads()
mgr.trace.query({ type, agent, after, limit })
```

### Queue Delivery
- 500ms polling loop checks all agent queues
- Idle agent → `session.prompt(message)`
- Busy agent + important → `session.steer(message)`
- Offline + persist → stays queued
- Offline + online-only → failed (still traced)

### Spike Code
- `spike-queue/agent-manager.ts` — core service API
- `spike-queue/message-queue.ts` — queue with threading
- `spike-queue/trace.ts` — audit log
- `spike-queue/test-queue.ts` — integration test
